---
title: "Exercise2_larose"
author: "Sam Scott"
date: "2026-03-03"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

bank <- read.csv('C:/Users/samsc/Desktop/ADS-Hands-On-Exercises/bank.csv', sep = ';')

#creating contingency table
contin_table <- table(bank$previous, bank$y)
contin_table

#get the first 9 records of the bank dataframe and save it as its own df
first_nine = bank[c(1:9), ]
first_nine

#get only age and marital status from the bank dataframe and save as its own df
age_marital = bank[c(1,3)]
age_marital

#get the first three rows of the age_martial df and save as its own df
first_three_am = age_marital[c(1:3),]
first_three_am


#Indexing in R and Python are quite different but the ideas are the same



adult <- read.csv('C:/Users/samsc/Desktop/ADS-502-Hands-On-Exercises/adult.csv')
adult

library(rpart)

#Create a contingency table of workclass and sex. Save output as table01.
table01 <- table(adult$sex, adult$workclass)
table01

#Create a contingenct table of marital status and sex. Save output as table02.
table02 <- table(adult$sex, adult$martial.status)
table02


#Display the sex and workclass values of the person in the first record. 
#What cell do they belong to? How many other records are the same as it?
first_adult <- adult[1,]
first_adult
first_adult_table <- table(first_adult$sex, first_adult$workclass)
first_adult_table

count <- table01[first_adult$sex, first_adult$workclass]
count

#Male, State-gov with 809 records with these two same attribute values

#Display the sex and marital status values of the people in records 6-10. Which
#cells of table02 do they belong to? How many other records in the data set have 
#the same combinations of sex and marital status values?

#CHATGPT assistance
six_thru_ten <- adult[c(6,7,8,9,10), ]
six_thru_ten
results <- data.frame(  
  sex = character(),
  marital_status = character(),
  count = numeric(),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

  for (i in 1:5) {
  sex_val <- six_thru_ten$sex[i]
  marital_val <- six_thru_ten$martial.status[i]
  count <- table02[sex_val, marital_val]
  results <- rbind(results, 
                  data.frame(sex = sex_val,
                            marital_status = marital_val,
                            count = count))
} 

results
